Making and breaking connections between proteins: beyond suppressor genetics?

Roger Brent

Department of Genetics
Harvard Medical School and
Department of Molecular Biology
Massachusetts General Hospital
50 Blossom Street
Boston, Massachusetts 02114


Every so often incremental changes in technology lead to large changes in how biologists go about their business. The advent of two hybrid schemes for detecting protein protein interactions is one such recent change. The continuing development of these techniques is taking us from the ability to identify individual important proteins to the ability to chart entire regulatory networks and disrupt the connections between their members.

In these systems, as is now well known, one typically begins with a known protein (the "bait") encoded by a cloned gene that is fused to a sequence encoding a DNA binding domain (Gal4 or LexA), and expressed in yeast containing target sites for Gal4 or LexA upstream of reporter genes. One then introduces into the strain a library of genes fused to a transcription activating moiety; a new protein (the "prey") that binds the bait will bring the activation moeity to the reporter genes and activate their transcription (figure 1). The inspiration for this came from natural examples of bait-prey interactions, such as the binding of the Herpes virus activator VP16 to Oct-1 (Triezenberg and McKnight, 1988) and form the use of chimeric proteins and specialized reporter genes to dissect transcription regulators and their interactions (Brent and Ptashne, 1985, Ma and Ptashne, 1988). Fields and Song (1989) described the first assay, in which interaction between proteins that were not transcription regulators brought an activation domain to a reporter, and thus uncoupled the study of interaction from the study of transcription itself. Reduction to practice took awhile, since it took time to devise systems where the transcription phenotypes were selectable, and to construct high quality interaction libraries (reviewed in Sternglanz and Fields, 1994).

Two main uses of this simple interaction technology have become well-established. The first is in interactor hunts to isolate new proteins from activator-tagged libraries that interact with LexA or GAL4 fusion protein baits (figure 1). The second is to measure specific protein-protein interactions in vivo, and to identify domains and structural features of proteins involved in these interactions. Since some current systems detect protein interactions weaker than those typically detected by co-immunopreciptiation experiments can be measured. Because the interactions occur inside a yeast nucleus, they occur at pH and salt concentrations that are physiologically relevant; at least for nuclear and cytoplasmic compartments. A salty environment, divalent cations, neutral pH: to paraphrase Monod, what is true in the S. cerevisiae nucleus may also be true in the elephant.

Artifacts and realities

The last few years have also seen the elaboration of cautionary doctrine for interpreting the results. This is necessary because not all detected interactions actually occur in nature. For example, proteins may associate using common motifs, such as amphipathic - helices, even though they never grace the same compartment of the same cells at the same time (Nathans, 1992). Common practice thus prescribes that a proposed interaction should be observed by a different technique, such as co-immunoprecipitation of the putative interactors from the appropriate cell or tissue type, which at least may be subject to different artifacts. But confirmation can also come from the two-hybrid technique itself. One criterion is suppression of an interaction defect: Hardy et al. (1992) found a protein, Rif1, that interacted with a wild type Rap1 bait and much less strongly with a missense mutant Rap1. A Rif1 mutant selected to restore interaction with the mutant Rap1 bait suppressed the effect of the Rap1 mutation. Another is specificity: it has long been evident that specific interaction of a protein with a given bait, but not with members of a panel of related and unrelated baits, lends credence to the idea that the interaction is real (Zervos et al., 1993). A third criterion is strength of interaction, which can be crudely estimated from interaction phenotypes when attention is paid to the stoichiometry of the interacting components (Gyuris et al., 1993). However, since so many important interactions in biology, including many enzyme substrate interactions and cooperative interactions between gene regulatory proteins, are weak, the correlation between strength of interaction and significance is imperfect at best.

Modified baits, multiprotein baits, and bridges

Some recent extensions of interaction technology are straightforward. One is a consequence of the fact that interacting proteins reside within living cells. When the proteins are modified postranslationally, the effect of the modification on interaction can be highly revealing. For example, Fagan et al. (1994) showed that the retinoblastoma gene product interacted with E2F-1 in yeast but not in vitro, which they showed on further analysis to be due to phosphorylation by a yeast Cyclin dependent kinase-- a phosphorylation that is mirrored in mammalian cells. Since many mammalian protein kinases can be expressed in yeast without killing the cell, interactions that depend on particular phosphorylation events are detectable. For example, interaction of the insulin receptor with its substrate requires that the LexA-receptor bait be an active kinase; when made as such a fusion, the receptor phosphoylates itself (O'Neill et all. 1994). Another extension is an outgrowth of the increasing facility with which a multiple exogenous proteins can be expressed in yeast, which has allowed the construction of multiprotein complexes on the DNA as baits. For example, Toyoshima and Hunter (1994) expressed a Gal4-Cdk4 fusion together with Cyclin D1 to create a two-component bait, which they used to isolate the Cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor p27. Similar approaches will likely allow isolation from expression libraries of proteins that bridge baits and interactors.

Connections and networks

Another, and perhaps more important development, has been the evolution of interaction as a genetic tool to reveal binary connections between known or newly isolated proteins and thus help explore genetic pathways. One of the first examples of this kind was the identification of Raf as a Ras interactor and a downstream effector of Ras function (Vojtek et al., 1993, Aelst et al, 1993, Avruch et al., 1993). This use invades the traditional provinces of suppressor genetics and of epistasis analysis, but provides overlapping information. Like suppressor genetics, two hybrid techniques can identify new partner proteins and suggest protein contacts (Hartman and Roth, 1971). Like epistasis analysis, interaction technology can assign proteins to pathways, but epistasis analysis can often suggest the order in which the proteins act (Avery and Wasserman 1992). which two hybrid techniques cannot, while two-hybrid techniques can indicate which pathway interactions involve protein contact, which epistasis analysis cannot.

But the real power of two-hybrid technology may lie in the ability to build individual binary links into more complex patterns of connections. One example of this is the ongoing efforts in many labs to isolate interactors with interactors, just as geneticists still isolate suppressors of suppressors. Another example is more subtle. Although initial epistasis analysis in S. cerevisiae suggested that Ste5 might act upstream of the Stell -> Ste7 -> Fus3/Kss1 MEKK/MEK/MAP protein kinase cascade, work by Choi et al. (1994) and Marcus et al. (1994) showed that these protein kinases interacted with Ste5, and Choi et al. showed additionally that Ste7. Stell, and Fus3/Kss1 interacted with independent sites on the Ste5 molecule. These facts suggested that Ste5 is a platform that holds the members of the kinase cascade and allows them to act on one another; that is, the pattern of interactions in the two-hybrid data suggested a new and hitherto unsuspected biochemical function for Ste5.

The use of interaction as a cartographic tool has been facilitated by the development of interaction mating techniques. In such techniques; putative interacting proteins are carried in haploid cells of opposite mating type, and the test is done by gridding the cells on plates, replica-mating them, and observing the transcription phenotypes in the matrix of resulting diploids (figure 2; Finley and Brent, 1994). Because these techniques allow hundreds of possible individual binary interactions to be tested, it is feasible to conduct them even when the test set of interacting components is not defined. As before, patterns of binary interactions can signal the existence of multiprotein complexes, and suggest functions for the interacting proteins; and as before, this is of particular importance in higher eukaryotic organisms which lack sophisticated genetics. For example, new proteins identified by genome sequencing efforts can be tested against panels of known proteins to help suggest function for them, and thus to guide further work. Moreover, the ability to perform large numbers of individual tests may enable identification of genes involved in disease: differential interaction of proteins with missense alleles of a disease state protein would suggest that they may function in the same process the disease affects (figure 3).

The incremental buildup, from individual binary links between proteins to more complex map of connections, may eventually reveal all possible connections between proteins, a gradualist approach to the protein linkage map envisioned by Bartel et al (1993). The utility of this knowledge will be greatly increased by a concurrent increase in the ability to manipulate specific protein interactions in vivo. This technology is also moving. At present, the only general tool for performing such manipulations is the expression of appropriate antibody derivatives inside cells, as recently reported by Chen et al. (1994); but in the future, it is likely that artificially selected peptides and nucleic acids will fill this role. Such ability may be particularly useful in vertebrates, in which manipulative genetics is rather primitive, in that it allow observation of the effects of elimination of individual protein protein interactions rather than individual proteins.

Given the massive increase in the number of important biological molecules, and the increased realization of the importance of transitory interactions between them, some shift of emphasis from the study of particular protein entities their connections was probably inevitable. It is likely to accelerate. This shift has its resonance in fields outside of biology: and it may be that tools developed to understand networks of inanimate computers will be useful in understanding networks of biological molecules. Continually improving physical and analytical means to study loose protein associations are invaluable: after all, a genetic regulatory network is only a protein machine which functions even though the pieces may be falling apart.

Figures. Figures 1. An interaction selection. Library-encoded protein, expressed in yeast as a chimeria that also contains activation domain, interacts in yeast with LexA or Gal4 fusion bait protein that is bound to DNA upstream of reporter genes. Proximity of the activation domain to the reporter genes causes them to be transcribed. Transcription of the LEU2 reporter causes the yeast to form a colony in the absence of Leucine. Transcription of the lacZ reporter causes the yeast to form a blue colony on Xgal medium (Gyuris et al., 1993).

Figure 2. An interaction mating. Horizontal streaks of haploid strains carrying different baits (top left) are mated with vertical streaks of haploid strains of the opposite mating type that carry putative interactors (top right). After replica mating, diploid colonies are selected (middle), replica plated to indicator medium (bottom left and right) and the transcription phenotypes scored (Finley and Brent, 1994).

Figure 3. Finley conjecture for identification of proteins involved in genetic diseases. Left. Four proteins interact with a protein involved in a disease; all are thus weak candidates for other proteins involved in the disease. Right. Two proteins interact differently with an allelic form of the protein that causes a disease state. These may function in the same process affected by the allele; and are thus stronger candidates for proteins involved in the disease, and the genes that encode them are candidate sites for genetic lesions (Finley and Brent, 1994).

References

Aelst, L. V., Barr, M., Marcus, S. Polverino, A., and Wigler, M. Complex formation between RAS and RAF and other protein kinases. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 1993, 90:6213-6217.

Avery, L. and Wasserman, S. Ordering gene function: the interpretation of epistasis in regulatory hierarchies. Trends in Genetics 1992, 8:312-316.

Bartel, P., Chien, C-T., Sternglanz, R., and Fields, S. 1993. Using the two-hybrid system to detect protein-protein interactions. In, Cellular interaction in development: a practical approach (D. A. Harley, Ed.) pp153-179. Oxford University Press, Oxford.

Brent, R. and Ptashne, M. A eukaryotic transcriptional activator bearing the DNA specificity of a prokaryotic repressor. Cell 1985, 43:729-736.

Chen, S-Y., Khouri, Y., Bagley, J., and Marasco, W. Combined intra- and extracellular immunization against human immunodeficiency virus type 1 infection with a human anti- gp120 antibody. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 1994, 91:5932-5936.

Chevray, P. M. and Nathans, D. Protein interaction cloning in yeast: identification of mammalian proteins that react with the leucine zipper of Jun. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 1992, 89:5789-5793.

Choi, K-Y., Satterberg, B., Lyons, D., and Elion, E. A. Ste5 tethers multiple protein kinases in the MAP kinase cascade required for mating in S. cerevisiae. Cell 1994, 78:499-512.

Fagan, R., Flint, K., and Jones, N. Phosphorylation of E2F-1 modulates its interaction with the Retinoblastoma gene product and the Adenoviral E4 19 kDa protein. Cell 1994, 78:799-811.

Fields, S. and Song, O-K. A novel genetic system to detect protein-protein interactions. Nature 1989, 340:245.

Fields, S. and Sternglanz, R. The two-hybrid system: an assay for protein-protein interactions. Trends in Genetics 1994, 10:286-292.

Finley Jr., R. and Brent, R. Interaction mating reveals binary and ternary connections between Drosophila cell cycle regulators. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 1994, in press.

Gyuris, J., Golemis, E., Chertkov, H., and Brent, B. Cdil, a human G1 and S phase protein phosphatase that associates with Cdk2. Cell 1993, 75:791-803.

Hardy, C. F. J., Sussel, L., and Shore, D. A RAP1-interacting protein involved in transcriptional silencing and telomere length regulation. Genes and Development 1992, 6:801-814.

Ma, J. and Ptashne, M. Converting a eukaryotic transcriptional inhibitor into an activator. Cell 1988, 55:443-446.

Marcus, S., Polverino, A., Barr, M., and Wigler, M. Complexes between STZ5 and components of the pheromone-responsive mitogen-activated protein kinase module. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 1994. 91:7762-7766.

O'Neill, T. J., Craparo, A., and Gustafson, T. A. Characterization of an interaction between insulin receptor substrate 1 and the insulin receptor by using the two-hybrid system. Mol. and Cell. Biol. 1994, 14, 6533-6442.

Toyoshima, H. and Hunter, T. p27, a novel inhibitor of G1 cyclin-Cdk protein kinase activity, is related to p21. Cell 1994, 78:67-74.

Triezenberg, S. J., LaMarco, K. L., McKnight, S. L. Evidence of DNA-protein interactions that medicate HSV-1 immediate early gene activation by VP16. Genes and Development 1988, 2:730-742.

Votjek, A., Hollenberg, S. and Cooper, J. Mammalian Ras interacts directly with the Serine/Threonine kinase Raf. Cell 1993, 74:205-214.

Zhang, X. F., Settleman, J., Kyriakis, J. M., Kateuchi-Suzuki, E., Elledge, S. J., Marshall, M. S., Bruder, J. T., Rapp, U. R., and Avruch, J. Normal and oncogenic p21ras proteins bind to the amino-terminal regulatory domain of c-Raf-1. Nature 1993, 364:308-313.

Zervos, A., Gyuris, J., and Brent, R. Mxi1, a protein that specifically interacts with Max to bind Myc-Max recognition sites. Cell 1993, 72:223-232.

 Reviews

Dear Amy:

I respond to your request for an evaluation of the Roger Brent minireview. The minireview has some positive and negative elements to it, to wit:

1. On the plus side is the fact that this represents a reasonably balanced, objective and fair overview of the field. While other sub-specialties take such for granted, the yeast two hybrid field is widely known to be driven by jealousies and small-mindedness. (I myself am not part of it!) Thus, this review represents a refreshing lack of confrontation and an open-minded generosity of Brent toward his competitors.

2. On the negative side is the fact that the minireview does not really say that much that is new. It is an overview of a field with which many if not most of Cell's readers will be familiar. There are a couple of minor excursions in novel directions, but they are not developed enough (in part because they represent still untested technology) to prove useful to those who might be interested in knowing more about the potentials of this technology.

3. On the few occasions when Brent does indeed venture briefly into the novel or the cutting edge, his prose is difficult to the point of being impenetrable, at least for those who don't already know what he wants to say before they read this ms. He tries to be clever and cute, but only ends up being obscure. The title is an example.


"Making and breaking connections between proteins: beyond suppressor genetics?" by Roger Brent

This is a pleasantly written minireview that has a strong accent on methodology. It focusses on the two-hybrid system for identifying protein-proein interactions and describes caustions in its use and several technical refinements on this technique such as the use of multiple baits and introduction of prey plasmids by mating. The review presents some new information from a theoretical perspective, but the concept of making "protein linkage" maps, as noted by the author, has already been published by Bartel et al. 1993. Because of the very strong methodological orientation to this review, I do not think that it is appropriate for Cell. Trends in Genetics or Current Biology would seem to be more suitable. In addition to the numberous typographical and spelling errors in the text, the following lists some other things that the author ought to consider in revising this interesting article for publication.

(1) page 1 and elsewhere: "protein-protein interactions"

(2) page 1: Triezenber et al., 1988

(3) page 5 and elsewhere: "two-hybrid techniques"

(4) page 6. The beautiful work of Printen and Sprague (1994) published in Genetics ought to be cited along with Choi et al. (1994) and Marcus et al. (1994).

(5) page 7: "may function in the same process the disease affects" is awkward and ought to be rewritten

(6) page 8: "entities to their connections..."

(7) legend to Figure 1. Fields and Song outght to be cited as well.

(8) legend to Figure 3. The term "Finley confecture" seems inappropriate and inflated.

(9) References: The first reference is Van Aelst et al (1993).
